title: Cron entry runs a downloader piped to a shell
explain: A cron entry's command ("{{ cron_entries.command }}", scheduled "{{ cron_entries.schedule }}" via {{ cron_entries.source }}) pipes a downloaded script straight into a shell. This pattern hides what actually runs, changes on the attacker's schedule, and leaves nothing on disk to inspect between runs.
fix: Review the cron entry, and if unrecognized, remove it and download/inspect the script manually before deciding whether it's legitimate.

title: Password maximum age is excessively long
explain: PASS_MAX_DAYS in /etc/login.defs is {{ login_defs.pass_max_days }} — passwords never expire in any practical sense, so a leaked credential stays valid indefinitely.
fix: Set 'PASS_MAX_DAYS 90' (or your organization's policy) in /etc/login.defs.

title: Password minimum age is zero
explain: PASS_MIN_DAYS in /etc/login.defs is 0 — a user forced to rotate a leaked password can immediately change it right back to the old (compromised) one, defeating the point of the forced rotation.
fix: Set 'PASS_MIN_DAYS 1' (or your organization's policy) in /etc/login.defs.

title: Password hashing rounds are not explicitly configured
explain: This host hashes passwords with SHA-512/256 (ENCRYPT_METHOD), but SHA_CRYPT_MIN_ROUNDS isn't set in /etc/login.defs, so new passwords get whatever the system's compiled-in default hashing cost is rather than a deliberately chosen one — fine until you actually need to raise the cost to keep pace with faster cracking hardware, at which point "never configured" means nobody has ever checked.
fix: Set 'SHA_CRYPT_MIN_ROUNDS 5000' (or higher, per current guidance) in /etc/login.defs.

title: Default UMASK is not explicitly configured
explain: No default umask is configured — neither an explicit UMASK line in /etc/login.defs nor a pam_umask entry in /etc/pam.d. Without a deliberate value, new files created by shells that don't set their own umask fall back to whatever the system default happens to be — commonly 022, which leaves new files group/world-readable by default rather than requiring an explicit opt-in to share them.
fix: Set 'UMASK 027' (or your organization's policy) in /etc/login.defs, or add pam_umask to the session stack.

title: Shell history recording is suppressed
explain: A shell startup file sets HISTSIZE=0, clears HISTFILE, or unsets it — commands run in that shell leave no trace in history. This is either a deliberate defense-evasion step by an attacker with a shell on the box, or a privacy tweak you made yourself; worth confirming which.
fix: If unrecognized, remove the HISTSIZE/HISTFILE override from ~/.bashrc, ~/.zshrc, or ~/.profile.

title: File modified since baseline: {{ file_integrity.path }}
explain: {{ file_integrity.path }} no longer matches its recorded baseline hash. This file is monitored because it directly controls authentication, privilege, or persistence on this host — an unexpected change here is exactly what credential theft, backdoor account creation, or PAM/sudo tampering looks like.
fix: Review the file's current contents against what you expect (version control, package manager, or your own memory of the last legitimate change). If the change wasn't yours, treat this as a likely compromise. If it was legitimate, run 'bulwarkctl fim baseline' to record the new state as the baseline.

title: File deleted since baseline: {{ file_integrity.path }}
explain: {{ file_integrity.path }} was present when the file-integrity baseline was recorded and is now missing. Deleting a security-critical file (rather than modifying it) is also a documented defense-evasion pattern — removing logs, PAM modules, or cron definitions to cover tracks or disable a control.
fix: Confirm whether the deletion was intentional (e.g. deliberate service removal). If not, restore the file from a package manager reinstall or backup, then run 'bulwarkctl fim baseline' once the host is back to a known-good state.

title: Not covered by the file-integrity baseline: {{ file_integrity.path }}
explain: {{ file_integrity.path }} is present but has no recorded baseline hash, so Bulwark can tell that it exists but not whether it has been tampered with. This fires per-file, on purpose: the previous version keyed on whether a baseline existed *at all*, which meant that once you had baselined anything, a critical file you had never actually recorded — including one added to the watch list later — read as "verified clean" rather than "never checked". A file nobody ever baselined is not a file known to be good.
fix: Run 'bulwarkctl fim baseline' now, while you trust this host, to record a known-good hash for this file (add '--privileged' to also cover /etc/shadow and /etc/sudoers).

title: Credential file modified since baseline: {{ file_integrity_privileged.path }}
explain: {{ file_integrity_privileged.path }} no longer matches its recorded baseline hash. This is one of the two root-only credential/privilege files Bulwark tracks (/etc/shadow, /etc/sudoers) — a change here is about as direct a signal of credential tampering or privilege-escalation setup as this host can produce.
fix: Review the change immediately. If it wasn't yours (a password reset via 'passwd', or a deliberate 'visudo' edit), treat this as a likely compromise. If legitimate, re-run 'sudo bulwarkctl fim baseline --privileged' to record the new known-good state.

title: Credential file deleted since baseline: {{ file_integrity_privileged.path }}
explain: {{ file_integrity_privileged.path }} was present at the last baseline and is now missing. A missing /etc/shadow or /etc/sudoers is either severe accidental damage or deliberate tampering — neither leaves this host in a safe state.
fix: Restore the file immediately from a package manager reinstall or backup — do not leave this host running without it. Then re-run 'sudo bulwarkctl fim baseline --privileged'.

title: Credential file not covered by the baseline: {{ file_integrity_privileged.path }}
explain: {{ file_integrity_privileged.path }} — one of the two root-only credential/privilege files Bulwark tracks (/etc/shadow, /etc/sudoers) — is present but has no baseline hash, so its integrity is unverifiable. This is the specific gap that made the old logic dangerous: a baseline recorded without '--privileged' never contains these two files, so a later privileged scan would hash them, find them "not baselined", and report nothing — the two most security-critical files on the host reading as clean when they had never actually been checked. Rated above info because these two files, unlike an ordinary config, are exactly where credential tampering hides.
fix: Run 'sudo bulwarkctl fim baseline --privileged' while you trust this host, to record a known-good hash for /etc/shadow and /etc/sudoers.

title: Could not verify file integrity: {{ file_integrity.path }}
explain: {{ file_integrity.path }} exists and is on the watch list, but Bulwark could not compute its hash this scan (sha256sum unavailable, or the file could not be read), so it cannot say whether the file matches its baseline. This is reported as its own state rather than being folded into "modified" — treating "I couldn't look" as "it changed" would raise a false critical alarm, and treating it as "unchanged" would hide a real one. Neither is honest.
fix: Check that the file is readable by the scanning user and that 'sha256sum' is installed and on PATH. If a privileged scan is needed to read it, run 'sudo bulwarkctl scan --privileged'.

title: Could not verify credential file: {{ file_integrity_privileged.path }}
explain: {{ file_integrity_privileged.path }} (/etc/shadow or /etc/sudoers) exists but its hash could not be computed this scan, so its integrity is unverifiable. Rated high because on the two most sensitive files Bulwark tracks, an inability to read them is itself worth attention — it can mean a permissions change that a privileged scan should have handled, or something actively interfering with access. It is deliberately not reported as "modified": a failure to look is not evidence of a change.
fix: Re-run under privilege ('sudo bulwarkctl scan --privileged') so the file is readable, and confirm 'sha256sum' is installed. If it still can't be read as root, investigate why.

title: A sensitive system file is world-writable
explain: "{{ file_permissions.path }}" is world-writable (mode {{ file_permissions.mode_octal }}). Any local user — or any process running as any user — can modify it directly.
fix: chmod o-w {{ file_permissions.path }}

title: /etc/shadow is world-readable
explain: /etc/shadow (mode {{ file_permissions.mode_octal }}) is readable by any local user. It holds every account's password hash — world-readable turns any local foothold into an offline password-cracking opportunity against every account on the box.
fix: chmod 640 /etc/shadow (or your distro's default — verify with 'stat /etc/shadow' on a known-good system).

title: ptrace scope allows tracing sibling processes
explain: ptrace is unrestricted: kernel.yama.ptrace_scope is 0 (or the Yama LSM isn't loaded at all, which the collector reports the same way, since without Yama there is no restriction to begin with). Any process can attach to and read the memory of any other process owned by the same user — including credential-scraping via /proc/<pid>/mem, a documented credential-access technique.
fix: Set 'kernel.yama.ptrace_scope=1' via /etc/sysctl.d/ and run 'sysctl --system'.

title: Kernel pointers are exposed to unprivileged users
explain: kernel.kptr_restrict is 0 — kernel pointer addresses in /proc are readable by any user, making kernel-exploit development easier (defeats KASLR for local attackers).
fix: Set 'kernel.kptr_restrict=1' (or 2) via /etc/sysctl.d/ and run 'sysctl --system'.

title: No mandatory access control framework is enforcing
explain: Neither AppArmor nor SELinux has any profile in enforcing mode. Without MAC, a compromised service runs with the full privileges of its process owner instead of being confined to what it actually needs. The 'enforcement_known' guard matters: AppArmor's profile list is readable only with CAP_MAC_ADMIN, so an unprivileged scan on a host where AppArmor is loaded cannot see whether it's enforcing. That is reported as undetermined, not as a finding — claiming "no MAC" because we lack the privilege to look would be alarming and wrong.
fix: On Debian/Ubuntu: 'sudo aa-enforce /etc/apparmor.d/*' for the profiles you want enforced, or install apparmor-profiles.

title: Address space layout randomization is disabled or partial
explain: kernel.randomize_va_space is {{ sysctl_kernel.randomize_va_space }}, not 2 (full ASLR). ASLR randomizes where the stack, heap, and shared libraries load in memory — without it (or with only partial randomization), memory-corruption exploits become dramatically easier to write reliably.
fix: Set 'kernel.randomize_va_space=2' via /etc/sysctl.d/ and run 'sysctl --system'.

title: Magic SysRq key is fully enabled
explain: kernel.sysrq exposes dangerous SysRq functions to anyone with keyboard or console access (physical, or a hijacked serial/BMC console). A value of 1 enables every function; a value above 1 is a bitmask, and this fires when that mask still includes the memory-dump bit (0x08, leaks kernel memory) or the signal bit (0x40, SIGKILL any process) — so a widened mask like 510 is caught, not just a plain 1, which the old exact-match condition missed.
fix: Set 'kernel.sysrq=176' (Ubuntu's restricted default) or '0' via /etc/sysctl.d/ and run 'sysctl --system'.

title: Unprivileged users can load BPF programs into the kernel
explain: kernel.unprivileged_bpf_disabled is 0 — any local user can load BPF programs into the kernel without CAP_SYS_ADMIN. The BPF verifier has been the source of numerous local privilege-escalation CVEs; this setting is what determines whether that class of bug is reachable by an unprivileged attacker at all.
fix: Set 'kernel.unprivileged_bpf_disabled=1' via /etc/sysctl.d/ and run 'sysctl --system'.

title: perf_event_paranoid allows unprivileged kernel profiling
explain: kernel.perf_event_paranoid is {{ sysctl_kernel.perf_event_paranoid }}, below 2 — the perf_event_open() syscall is a documented side-channel and kernel-address-disclosure vector, and several real privilege-escalation exploits have used it once accessible to unprivileged users.
fix: Set 'kernel.perf_event_paranoid=2' (or higher) via /etc/sysctl.d/ and run 'sysctl --system'.

title: kexec can load an unsigned, arbitrary kernel image
explain: kernel.kexec_load_disabled is 0 — a process with sufficient privilege can use kexec to load and immediately switch execution to a completely different, unverified kernel image, bypassing secure boot and any measured-boot chain entirely, to survive a reboot with a backdoored kernel. This fires only when kernel lockdown is off: with Secure Boot enabled the kernel runs in lockdown 'integrity' mode, which already refuses an unsigned kexec image regardless of this flag, so the flag is defense-in-depth there rather than a gap — and the rule stays quiet. Rated low because loading a kexec image already requires CAP_SYS_BOOT (root); this hardens against a post-compromise persistence step.
fix: Set 'kernel.kexec_load_disabled=1' via /etc/sysctl.d/ and run 'sysctl --system'. Note this disables kdump/crash-dump capture and 'systemctl kexec'; and once it is set to 1 at runtime it cannot be returned to 0 without a reboot (the sysctl.d drop-in itself re-applies every boot).

title: Core dumps of setuid processes are not restricted
explain: fs.suid_dumpable is 1 ("debuggable") — a crashed setuid/setgid process dumps core as a file the invoking (unprivileged) user can read, which can leak secrets that were in that process's memory (keys, passwords, tokens) to a lower-privileged user. This is distinct from the safer "0" (no dump) or "2" (routed through a controlled dump handler like systemd-coredump/apport) settings.
fix: Set 'fs.suid_dumpable=0' via /etc/sysctl.d/ and run 'sysctl --system'.

title: Hardlink creation protection is disabled
explain: fs.protected_hardlinks is 0 — any user can create a hardlink to a file they don't own (e.g. a setuid binary or a file another process will later run as root), a classic TOCTOU/race primitive for local privilege escalation.
fix: Set 'fs.protected_hardlinks=1' via /etc/sysctl.d/ and run 'sysctl --system'.

title: Symlink following protection is disabled
explain: fs.protected_symlinks is 0 — a process can follow a symlink in a world-writable sticky directory (like /tmp) even when it doesn't own the link, a long-standing local-privilege-escalation and file-clobbering primitive.
fix: Set 'fs.protected_symlinks=1' via /etc/sysctl.d/ and run 'sysctl --system'.

title: FIFO creation protection in sticky directories is disabled
explain: fs.protected_fifos is 0 — a process can write to a FIFO it doesn't own inside a world-writable sticky directory, which has been used to intercept or tamper with data another (possibly privileged) process expects to read back.
fix: Set 'fs.protected_fifos=1' (or 2 for stricter mode) via /etc/sysctl.d/ and run 'sysctl --system'.

title: Regular-file creation protection in sticky directories is disabled
explain: fs.protected_regular is 0 — a process can open a regular file it doesn't own inside a world-writable sticky directory in write mode, the same class of interception/tampering risk as the FIFO case but for plain files.
fix: Set 'fs.protected_regular=1' (or 2 for stricter mode) via /etc/sysctl.d/ and run 'sysctl --system'.

title: ICMP redirects are accepted
explain: net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_redirects is 1 — this host will update its routing table based on unauthenticated ICMP redirect packets from anyone on the local network, a classic man-in-the-middle primitive for redirecting traffic through an attacker-controlled host.
fix: Set 'net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_redirects=0' via /etc/sysctl.d/ and run 'sysctl --system'.

title: IP source routing is enabled
explain: net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_source_route is 1 — this host accepts packets that specify their own route, letting a remote attacker bypass normal routing/firewall path assumptions and spoof traffic as if it originated from a trusted internal address.
fix: Set 'net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_source_route=0' via /etc/sysctl.d/ and run 'sysctl --system'.

title: This host sends ICMP redirects
explain: net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects is 1 — a non-router host has no legitimate reason to send ICMP redirects, and leaving this on is unnecessary route-manipulation surface.
fix: Set 'net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects=0' via /etc/sysctl.d/ and run 'sysctl --system' (skip this if the host is intentionally acting as a router).

title: Martian packet logging is disabled
explain: net.ipv4.conf.all.log_martians is 0 — packets with impossible/spoofed source addresses (no route back to the claimed source) are silently dropped instead of logged, losing an early signal of spoofing or scanning activity on the network.
fix: Set 'net.ipv4.conf.all.log_martians=1' via /etc/sysctl.d/ and run 'sysctl --system'.

title: Reverse-path filtering is disabled
explain: net.ipv4.conf.all.rp_filter is 0 — the kernel will forward packets whose source address couldn't possibly have arrived via the interface they came in on, which is exactly the condition IP spoofing depends on. (A value of 1 or 2 — strict or loose mode — is fine; this only fires when filtering is fully off.)
fix: Set 'net.ipv4.conf.all.rp_filter=2' (loose mode) via /etc/sysctl.d/ and run 'sysctl --system'. Use '1' (strict) only on single-homed hosts — strict reverse-path filtering drops legitimate traffic on multi-homed servers and any asymmetric-routing setup, which is exactly the server class most likely to be affected, so loose is the safe default that still blocks spoofing.

title: TCP SYN cookies are disabled
explain: net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies is 0 — this host's TCP stack has no fallback protection against a SYN-flood connection-table exhaustion denial of service.
fix: Set 'net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies=1' via /etc/sysctl.d/ and run 'sysctl --system'.

title: Kernel module not blacklisted: {{ module_blacklist.module }}
explain: The '{{ module_blacklist.module }}' kernel module isn't blacklisted in /etc/modprobe.d/, meaning it can still be auto-loaded on demand — simply by an unprivileged process opening a socket of that family. All four of these network protocol modules (dccp, sctp, rds, tipc) have a documented history of local-privilege-escalation vulnerabilities reachable exactly that way, and essentially no host actually uses them. usb-storage is deliberately NOT covered here — it's a different threat (physical-media exfiltration/BadUSB, not remote exploitation) and the remediation is destructive on a workstation, so it lives in BLWK-KERNEL-021 behind the server profile.
fix: If '{{ module_blacklist.module }}' isn't actually needed on this host, add 'install {{ module_blacklist.module }} /bin/true' to a file under /etc/modprobe.d/ (e.g. /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist-rare.conf) and run 'update-initramfs -u'.

title: Kernel module not blacklisted: usb-storage
explain: The 'usb-storage' module isn't blacklisted, so plugging in a USB mass-storage device on this host will auto-mount it. On a server — especially one in a rack you don't physically control, like a colo or a branch office — that's a data-exfiltration and BadUSB path that needs no credentials and leaves little trace: someone with thirty seconds of physical access walks away with the disk contents. This rule is scoped to the server profile on purpose. The same advice on a laptop or desktop is actively harmful — it breaks every USB stick, external drive, and SD reader the user owns, to defend against an attacker who by definition already has their hands on the machine. Split out of BLWK-KERNEL-020 for exactly that reason.
fix: On a server with no legitimate need for USB storage, add 'install usb-storage /bin/true' to a file under /etc/modprobe.d/ (e.g. /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist-usb-storage.conf) and run 'update-initramfs -u'. Do NOT apply this to a workstation — you will break all removable media on it.

title: No custom legal warning banner is set (/etc/{{ banners.file }})
explain: /etc/{{ banners.file }} still contains the distro's default auto-generated content (OS name and getty templating codes), not a deliberate legal warning. In some jurisdictions, a clear "unauthorized access is prohibited" banner is what establishes that someone connecting without authorization was actually warned — the default OS-identification banner doesn't do that, and also unnecessarily advertises the exact OS version to anyone who connects. Scoped to the server profile: a login banner's function is legal, not technical — it establishes that access was unauthorized, which matters for a multi-user remote system. On a personal desktop it protects nothing, and /etc/issue.net is read only by sshd, so on a host that doesn't run sshd it is inert text in a file nobody opens.
fix: Replace the contents of /etc/issue (local login) and /etc/issue.net (remote login) with a warning banner, not OS version info.

title: auditd is not installed
explain: No auditd binary was found. Without it, there's no kernel-level record of privileged syscalls (file access, exec, permission changes) — the trail that makes a break-in reviewable after the fact instead of just a guess.
fix: Install auditd (e.g. 'apt install auditd') and enable it with 'systemctl enable --now auditd'.

title: System logs are not forwarded off-box
explain: rsyslog has no remote forwarding rule configured — logs only exist locally. Anyone with root on this machine can edit or delete them, so local-only logging can't be trusted as evidence of what a root-level attacker did. A workstation has no central syslog to ship to, so this is scoped to the server profile; the point of off-box logs is that an attacker who roots the machine can't erase the record of how they got in, which only means something where someone is actually doing IR.
fix: Add a forwarding rule to /etc/rsyslog.conf (e.g. '*.* @@logs.example.com:514') pointing at a log destination this machine doesn't control.

title: Kernel-level process accounting is not installed
explain: Neither 'accton' nor 'lastcomm' is present, so there's no kernel-level record of every process that ran on this host, who ran it, and how long it took — a different, harder- to-tamper-with data source than shell history or application logs, since it's written by the kernel itself rather than by the process being recorded.
fix: Install process accounting (e.g. 'apt install acct') and enable it with 'systemctl enable --now acct'.

title: A VNC/remote-desktop port is listening
explain: Port {{ listening_ports.port }} is listening on {{ listening_ports.listen_address }} — a standard VNC (5900-range) or noVNC/websockify (6080) port, reachable from the network. If this wasn't deliberately set up with a password and restricted access, it's a full remote-desktop backdoor. A listener bound only to loopback (127.0.0.1/::1) is deliberately excluded: that's the normal SSH-tunnel / container-published pattern and isn't remotely reachable.
fix: If this isn't an intentional remote-desktop setup: stop the service (check 'ps aux' for x11vnc/vncserver/websockify) and remove any systemd unit or cron entry that restarts it.

title: A port commonly used by ngrok's local API is listening
explain: Port 4040 is listening on {{ listening_ports.listen_address }} — most often ngrok's local web-inspector/API port, present while an ngrok tunnel is active. It's also Apache Spark's default UI port and is used by a few other dev tools, so this isn't conclusive on its own: if you're not running ngrok, check what it is. A loopback-only 4040 (the usual case for both ngrok and Spark) is excluded — this fires only when it's exposed on the network.
fix: Check 'ps aux | grep ngrok' and 'systemctl list-units | grep -i ngrok' to find what started it.

title: systemd unit runs a tunneling tool
explain: The systemd unit "{{ systemd_units.unit_name }}" runs a command referencing a tunneling tool ("{{ systemd_units.exec_start }}"). A reverse tunnel that reaches back out through a service like ngrok or cloudflared survives reboots and bypasses inbound firewall rules entirely, since it's an outbound connection — which is why it's both a legitimate way to expose a service and a common attacker persistence pattern. The two look identical from here, so this is high rather than critical: if you deliberately installed cloudflared, autossh, or a similar tunnel, this is expected and you can accept the risk for this rule.
fix: If you didn't set this up yourself: 'systemctl disable --now <unit>' and remove the unit file from /etc/systemd/system/, then rotate any credentials the box had access to.

title: systemd unit posts to a messaging API on start
explain: The systemd unit "{{ systemd_units.unit_name }}" shells out to curl against a chat/ messaging API on start ("{{ systemd_units.exec_start }}"). This exact pattern is used to notify an attacker (e.g. of a new SSH tunnel URL) every time the service restarts — treat it as exfiltration infrastructure unless you recognize it as your own notification script.
fix: Review the unit file under /etc/systemd/system/ and disable it if you didn't create it.

title: Unexpected LaunchAgent/LaunchDaemon found
explain: The LaunchAgent/LaunchDaemon "{{ launchd_persistence.label }}" runs on every login/boot and isn't one this host's baseline recognizes — macOS's equivalent of a rogue systemd unit.
fix: Review the plist under ~/Library/LaunchAgents or /Library/Launch{Agents,Daemons} and remove it if you didn't create it.

title: Unexpected registry Run-key entry found
explain: The registry Run-key entry "{{ windows_run_keys.name }}" launches "{{ windows_run_keys.command }}" on every login and isn't one this host's baseline recognizes — Windows's equivalent of a rogue systemd unit.
fix: Review the entry under HKLM/HKCU ...\CurrentVersion\Run and remove it if you didn't create it.

title: A sudoers entry allows passwordless root
explain: A NOPASSWD sudo rule in {{ sudoers.source }} ("{{ sudoers.line }}") lets that user/group run commands as root without re-authenticating — anyone who reaches that account, even briefly, has unrestricted root with no password prompt to slow them down.
fix: Review the entry and remove NOPASSWD unless there's a specific, scoped reason for it (e.g. a single monitoring command, not 'ALL').

title: No GRUB boot loader password is set
explain: /boot/grub/grub.cfg has no 'password' or 'password_pbkdf2' directive. Anyone with physical or console access to this machine can interrupt the boot menu and edit kernel parameters directly (e.g. appending 'init=/bin/bash' or 'single') to get a root shell without ever needing a valid credential — no OS-level control can prevent this once someone has unrestricted access to the boot menu itself.
fix: Set a GRUB password: run 'grub-mkpasswd-pbkdf2' to generate a hash, add 'set superusers="root"' and 'password_pbkdf2 root <hash>' to /etc/grub.d/40_custom, then run 'update-grub'. IMPORTANT: on stock Ubuntu/Debian, 'set superusers' makes GRUB prompt for that password before booting ANY entry — the machine will halt at a password prompt on every reboot, including unattended ones. To keep normal boot automatic and only gate *editing* entries and recovery, append '--unrestricted' to the CLASS list of your default menuentry (edit /etc/grub.d/10_linux, or use a submenu). Test with a reboot you can physically recover from before relying on this on a headless host.

title: ClamAV is not installed
explain: No clamscan binary was found. Bulwark deliberately doesn't reimplement signature-based malware detection (see the project's own non-goals) — it expects ClamAV to cover that ground, so without it this whole category goes unchecked.
fix: Install ClamAV (e.g. 'apt install clamav') and run 'freshclam' to fetch signatures.

title: ClamAV signature database is stale
explain: ClamAV's signature database is {{ clamav_status.db_age_days }} days old. An installed but outdated scanner gives a false sense of coverage — it won't catch anything discovered since the last update.
fix: Run 'freshclam' (or check that the freshclam timer/service is enabled: 'systemctl status clamav-freshclam').

title: A network interface is running in promiscuous mode
explain: {{ network_interfaces.interface }} is in promiscuous mode — it's capturing all traffic on its network segment, not just traffic addressed to this host. This is the defining behavior of a packet sniffer, and is exactly what chkrootkit's own "sniffer" test checks for. Bridge ports are excluded: the kernel makes every bridge-enslaved interface promiscuous by design, so a Docker/VM veth being promiscuous means nothing. This interface is not one of those.
fix: Run 'ip -d link show {{ network_interfaces.interface }}' and check 'ps aux' for a legitimate reason (tcpdump, Wireshark/tshark). If nothing legitimate explains it, treat this as active traffic interception and investigate immediately.

title: SSH password authentication is enabled
explain: PasswordAuthentication is set to "{{ sshd.password_authentication }}" in sshd_config, which allows an attacker to brute-force their way in with a password guess instead of needing a private key. It is the OpenSSH default and a brute-force *exposure* rather than a definite compromise, so it's rated high (aligning with CIS's L1 treatment and with BLWK-SSH-002), not critical.
fix: Set 'PasswordAuthentication no' in /etc/ssh/sshd_config and run 'systemctl restart sshd'. IMPORTANT: confirm key-based login works in a SECOND session before you rely on this — if the only working credential is a password and no key is installed, you will lock yourself out on the next login. Existing sessions survive the restart; a new key-authenticated login must succeed first.

title: SSH root login is permitted
explain: PermitRootLogin is set to "{{ sshd.permit_root_login }}", allowing direct root logins over SSH. A compromised root password or key is a single step from full system control.
fix: Set 'PermitRootLogin no' (or 'prohibit-password' if key-based root access is required) in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.

title: SSH allows empty passwords
explain: PermitEmptyPasswords is set to "{{ sshd.permit_empty_passwords }}" — any account with a blank password can log in over SSH with no credential at all.
fix: Set 'PermitEmptyPasswords no' in /etc/ssh/sshd_config and run 'systemctl restart sshd'.

title: SSH X11 forwarding is enabled
explain: X11Forwarding is set to "{{ sshd.x11_forwarding }}" in sshd_config. X11 forwarding exposes any local X server to whatever runs on the remote end of the session, and has a history of X11-protocol-level compromise (keystroke injection, screen capture).
fix: Set 'X11Forwarding no' in /etc/ssh/sshd_config and run 'systemctl restart sshd', unless remote GUI apps are a deliberate requirement.

title: SSH TCP forwarding/tunneling is enabled
explain: AllowTcpForwarding is set to "{{ sshd.allow_tcp_forwarding }}" in sshd_config. TCP forwarding lets any SSH user open arbitrary local or remote port tunnels through this host — the exact mechanism used to pivot into internal networks or to reach services (like VNC) that are only supposed to be reachable from localhost.
fix: Set 'AllowTcpForwarding no' in /etc/ssh/sshd_config, or restrict it per-user with a Match block, unless tunneling is a deliberate requirement.

title: SSH permits user-set environment variables
explain: PermitUserEnvironment is set to "{{ sshd.permit_user_environment }}" in sshd_config, letting an SSH client set arbitrary environment variables for its session (via ~/.ssh/environment or SendEnv) — including ones like LD_PRELOAD that can hijack the behavior of programs the session later runs.
fix: Set 'PermitUserEnvironment no' in /etc/ssh/sshd_config and run 'systemctl restart sshd'.

title: SSH tun-device (layer 2/3) tunneling is enabled
explain: PermitTunnel is set to "{{ sshd.permit_tunnel }}" in sshd_config. Unlike ordinary TCP port forwarding, tun-device tunneling gives an SSH client a full virtual network interface into this host's network — a much broader pivot/VPN-like capability that's rarely needed and should be off by default.
fix: Set 'PermitTunnel no' in /etc/ssh/sshd_config and run 'systemctl restart sshd'.

title: SSH StrictModes is disabled
explain: StrictModes is set to "{{ sshd.strict_modes }}" in sshd_config. With StrictModes off, sshd no longer verifies that a user's home directory and key files have safe ownership and permissions before trusting them — a world-writable home directory or key file then becomes a usable login bypass instead of a rejected, obviously-broken config.
fix: Set 'StrictModes yes' in /etc/ssh/sshd_config and run 'systemctl restart sshd'.

title: SSH GatewayPorts exposes forwarded ports to the network
explain: GatewayPorts is set to "{{ sshd.gateway_ports }}" in sshd_config. By default, a port an SSH client forwards to this host is only reachable from localhost; with GatewayPorts on, it's reachable from anywhere on the network — turning an individual user's ad-hoc SSH tunnel into an accidental (or deliberate) network-wide exposure of whatever it points at.
fix: Set 'GatewayPorts no' in /etc/ssh/sshd_config and run 'systemctl restart sshd'.

title: SSH agent forwarding is enabled
explain: AllowAgentForwarding is set to "{{ sshd.allow_agent_forwarding }}" in sshd_config. Agent forwarding lets anyone with root (or the forwarding user's privileges) on this host make use of the connecting client's SSH agent to authenticate onward as that client, without ever seeing the private key itself — a well-known lateral-movement technique on multi-hop SSH setups.
fix: Set 'AllowAgentForwarding no' in /etc/ssh/sshd_config unless agent forwarding is a deliberate requirement.

title: SSH allows an excessive number of authentication attempts per connection
explain: MaxAuthTries is set to {{ sshd.max_auth_tries }} in sshd_config — well above the recommended ceiling of 3-6. A high limit gives an online password-guessing attempt many more tries per TCP connection before being cut off.
fix: Set 'MaxAuthTries 3' (or up to 6) in /etc/ssh/sshd_config and run 'systemctl restart sshd'.

title: SSH private key is not passphrase-protected
explain: The SSH private key at {{ ssh_private_keys.path }} (format: {{ ssh_private_keys.key_format }}) has no passphrase, so it is a plaintext credential on disk. Anyone who obtains a copy of that one file — from a backup, a lost or stolen laptop, a dotfiles repo synced to the cloud, or any process running as you — can authenticate as you to every server that trusts it, immediately and with nothing else required. A passphrase does not stop a determined attacker forever, but it turns "copy one file and log in" into "also crack a passphrase," and it is what lets you notice and rotate the key before it is used.
fix: Add a passphrase to the existing key in place (this keeps the same key, so you do not have to re-add it to any server): run 'ssh-keygen -p -f {{ ssh_private_keys.path }}' and enter a strong passphrase when prompted. To avoid typing it on every use, load the key into your agent once per session with 'ssh-add {{ ssh_private_keys.path }}'. If this key is shared, checked into a repo, or you are unsure who has copied it, treat it as compromised: generate a fresh one with 'ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -a 100', deploy the new public key, and remove the old key from every server's authorized_keys.

title: SSH private key passphrase status could not be determined
explain: The file at {{ ssh_private_keys.path }} (format: {{ ssh_private_keys.key_format }}) is a private key, but its encryption header could not be parsed, so Bulwark cannot confirm whether it is passphrase-protected. Rather than guess — and risk reporting a plaintext key as safe — it is surfaced here for you to check by hand. A healthy key does not normally hit this state, so the file may be truncated, corrupted, or in a format this version doesn't fully recognize.
fix: Inspect the key directly: 'ssh-keygen -y -f {{ ssh_private_keys.path }}' will prompt for a passphrase if one is set (and error out plainly if the file is malformed). If it is unprotected, add a passphrase with 'ssh-keygen -p -f {{ ssh_private_keys.path }}'. If the file is corrupt or not actually a key you use, remove or restore it.
